name: Curcumin
note: Natural yellow pigment from turmeric. EFSA found no safety concern at typical intakes.

name: Riboflavin
note: Vitamin B2 used as a yellow color. No safety concern.

name: Tartrazine
note: Azo dye. In the EU, products containing it must carry a warning that it may adversely affect activity and attention in children. Widely used in US drinks, candy and snacks. The US FDA announced a voluntary industry phase-out of this dye targeted for the end of 2026.
maxPermittedLevel: 100 mg/L in soft drinks (EU); child-attention warning label required

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Designated as Food Yellow No. 4

name: Quinoline Yellow
note: Synthetic dye with an EU child-attention warning label and sharply reduced permitted levels. Not approved for food in the US, Canada or Japan.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not on Japan's designated or existing additive lists
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not on Health Canada List 3 (permitted colours)

name: Sunset Yellow FCF
note: Azo dye requiring the EU warning about effects on activity and attention in children. Very common in US snacks and drinks. The US FDA announced a voluntary industry phase-out of this dye targeted for the end of 2026.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Designated as Food Yellow No. 5

name: Cochineal / Carmine
note: Red pigment from cochineal insects. Safe for most people but can trigger allergic reactions in a small minority.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Cochineal extract is listed; carmine lake itself is not designated

name: Azorubine (Carmoisine)
note: Azo dye with the EU child-attention warning requirement. Not permitted in the US, Canada or Japan.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not on Japan's designated or existing additive lists
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not on Health Canada List 3 (permitted colours)

name: Ponceau 4R
note: Azo dye with the EU child-attention warning requirement. Not permitted in the US or Canada.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Designated as Food Red No. 102 (New Coccine)
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not listed; Canada permits only Ponceau SX, a different dye

name: Erythrosine
note: Iodine-containing dye linked to thyroid tumors in animal studies. The EU allows it only in cocktail cherries; the US FDA moved to revoke it from foods in 2025 but it remains in many products. US food uses must end by January 15, 2027.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Designated as Food Red No. 3

name: Allura Red AC
note: The most common red dye in US foods. In the EU it requires a warning that it may adversely affect activity and attention in children. The US FDA announced a voluntary industry phase-out of this dye targeted for the end of 2026.
maxPermittedLevel: 100 mg/L in soft drinks (EU); child-attention warning label required

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Designated as Food Red No. 40

name: Patent Blue V
note: Synthetic blue dye, occasionally linked to allergic reactions. Not permitted in the US, Canada or Japan.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not on Japan's designated additive list
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not on Health Canada List 3 (permitted colours)

name: Indigotine
note: Synthetic blue dye. EFSA re-evaluation found no major concern at permitted levels.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Designated as Food Blue No. 2 (Indigo Carmine)

name: Brilliant Blue FCF
note: Synthetic blue dye. EFSA found no safety concern at current use levels.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Designated as Food Blue No. 1

name: Chlorophylls
note: Natural green plant pigment. No safety concern.

name: Copper chlorophyllins
note: Stabilized form of chlorophyll. No safety concern at permitted levels.

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Designated; limited to kombu/seaweed, fruit-veg preserves, gum, candy, agar
regionStatus.Canada: Restricted: Sodium copper chlorophyllin listed only for flavour essences and extracts

name: Green S
note: Synthetic green dye with limited toxicology data. Not permitted in the US or Canada.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not on Japan's designated additive list
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not listed; Fast Green FCF is Canada's permitted green dye

name: Plain caramel
note: Caramel made by heating sugars without reactants. No safety concern.

name: Caustic sulphite caramel
note: Caramel color made with sulphite compounds. Minor concerns for very high consumers.

name: Ammonia caramel
note: Caramel made with ammonium compounds; can contain the by-product 4-MEI, which is under scrutiny as a possible carcinogen.

name: Sulphite ammonia caramel
note: The caramel color in most colas. Can contain 4-MEI, a possible carcinogen that California requires warnings for above certain levels.

name: Vegetable carbon
note: Black color from carbonized plant material. EFSA flagged data gaps around nanoparticles. Not permitted in US food.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Listed as vegetable carbon black (existing additives list)
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Permitted as carbon black, unlike the US

name: Brown HT
note: Synthetic brown azo dye. Not permitted in the US, Canada or Japan.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not on Japan's designated additive list
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not on Health Canada List 3 (permitted colours)

name: Carotenes
note: Orange pigment also found in carrots. No safety concern as a food color.

name: Annatto
note: Natural orange color from achiote seeds. Rare allergic-type reactions reported.

name: Paprika extract
note: Natural red-orange color from paprika. No safety concern.

name: Lycopene
note: Red pigment also found in tomatoes. No safety concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Listed as tomato colour (existing additives list)
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Listed as lycopene extract from tomato

name: Lutein
note: Yellow pigment from marigolds, also found in leafy greens. No safety concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Listed as marigold colour (existing additives list)

name: Beetroot red
note: Natural red color from beets. No safety concern.

name: Anthocyanins
note: Natural purple-red pigments from fruits and vegetables. No safety concern.

name: Calcium carbonate
note: Chalk mineral used as a white color and anti-caking agent. No safety concern.

name: Titanium dioxide
note: White pigment BANNED in EU foods since 2022 after EFSA concluded genotoxicity could not be ruled out (nanoparticle accumulation). Still legal and common in US candy, gum, frosting and pills.
maxPermittedLevel: None (removed from the EU list in 2022)

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Still permitted in Japan (banned in EU 2022)
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Still permitted in Canada (banned in EU 2022)

name: Iron oxides
note: Mineral pigments (yellow, red, black). No safety concern at permitted levels.

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Iron sesquioxide use limited to konjac and banana stems

name: Aluminium
note: Metallic surface color allowed only for specific uses; contributes to total aluminium exposure, which EFSA advises limiting.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Metallic aluminium not on Japan's positive list

name: Sorbic acid
note: Common preservative against mold. Considered low concern; rare skin-type reactions.

name: Potassium sorbate
note: Very common mold-inhibiting preservative. Low concern at permitted levels.

name: Calcium sorbate
note: Sorbate preservative. Low concern at permitted levels.

name: Benzoic acid
note: Preservative that can form traces of benzene in acidic drinks that also contain vitamin C, and can trigger reactions in sensitive people.

name: Sodium benzoate
note: Widespread preservative in sodas and condiments. Can form traces of benzene with vitamin C and was part of the mix in the Southampton hyperactivity study.

name: Potassium benzoate
note: Benzoate preservative with the same benzene-formation concern as sodium benzoate.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Only benzoic acid and sodium benzoate are designated

name: Sulfur dioxide
note: Preservative in dried fruit and wine. A significant allergen for sulfite-sensitive people and asthmatics; must be declared on labels.

name: Sodium sulfite
note: Sulfite preservative; can trigger reactions in sulfite-sensitive people.

name: Sodium bisulfite
note: Sulfite preservative; can trigger reactions in sulfite-sensitive people.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Sodium bisulfite not separately designated in Japan

name: Sodium metabisulfite
note: Sulfite preservative; can trigger reactions in sulfite-sensitive people.

name: Potassium metabisulfite
note: Sulfite preservative; can trigger reactions in sulfite-sensitive people.

name: Potassium bisulfite
note: Sulfite preservative; can trigger reactions in sulfite-sensitive people.

name: Nisin
note: Natural antibacterial peptide used in some cheeses. Low concern at permitted levels.

name: Natamycin
note: Antifungal used on cheese rinds and cured meat surfaces. Low concern as a surface treatment.

name: Dimethyl dicarbonate
note: Cold-sterilization agent for drinks that breaks down after use. Low concern.

name: Potassium nitrite
note: Curing salt for meats. Nitrites can form nitrosamines, some of which are carcinogenic; the EU tightened permitted levels in 2023.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Only sodium nitrite designated; potassium nitrite not listed

name: Sodium nitrite
note: The main curing agent in bacon, ham and deli meat. Linked to nitrosamine formation; processed meat is classified as carcinogenic to humans by IARC. The EU cut permitted levels in 2023. The lower EU limits apply since October 9, 2025 (Regulation 2023/2108).
maxPermittedLevel: 80 mg/kg ingoing in most cured meats (EU, 2023 revision)

name: Sodium nitrate
note: Curing agent that converts to nitrite in the body. Same nitrosamine concern as nitrites; EU levels were tightened in 2023.

name: Potassium nitrate
note: Traditional curing salt with the same nitrosamine concerns as other nitrates/nitrites.

name: Acetic acid
note: The acid in vinegar. No safety concern.

name: Lactic acid
note: Acid produced by fermentation. No safety concern.

name: Propionic acid
note: Mold inhibitor in baked goods. Generally low concern; some research interest in high-intake metabolic effects.

name: Sodium propionate
note: Propionate mold inhibitor for baked goods. Low concern at typical levels.

name: Calcium propionate
note: Very common bread preservative. Low concern at typical levels.

name: Potassium propionate
note: Propionate mold inhibitor. Low concern at typical levels.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not on MHLW Designated Additives list (only Ca/Na propionate permitted)
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not on Health Canada permitted preservatives list

name: Carbon dioxide
note: Carbonation gas. No safety concern.

name: Malic acid
note: Fruit acid also found in apples. No safety concern.

name: Fumaric acid
note: Acidulant. No safety concern at permitted levels.

name: Propylparaben
note: Paraben preservative REMOVED from the EU approved list in 2006 over effects on sex hormones in animal studies. Still permitted in some US baked goods and tortillas.
maxPermittedLevel: None (removed from the EU list in 2006)

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Permitted only in fruit sauce, soy sauce, vinegar, fruit/veg skins, wine
regionStatus.Canada: Restricted: Permitted only in antifoaming preps, colour formulations, enzyme preps

name: Methylparaben
note: Paraben preservative still permitted in the EU within limits. Lower concern than propylparaben.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not designated in Japan; only ethyl/propyl/isopropyl/butyl/isobutyl parabens permitted
regionStatus.Canada: Restricted: Permitted only in antifoaming preps, colour formulations, enzyme preps

name: Ascorbic acid
note: Vitamin C used as an antioxidant. No safety concern.

name: Sodium ascorbate
note: Salt of vitamin C. No safety concern.

name: Ascorbyl palmitate
note: Fat-soluble form of vitamin C. No safety concern.

name: Tocopherol-rich extract
note: Natural vitamin E antioxidant. No safety concern.

name: Alpha-tocopherol
note: Synthetic vitamin E antioxidant. No safety concern.

name: Propyl gallate
note: Synthetic antioxidant for fats with debated endocrine and toxicology data.

name: TBHQ
note: Synthetic antioxidant very common in US snack foods and frying oils. Animal studies at high doses raised tumor and immune questions; EFSA set a strict acceptable intake. Not on Japan's positive list of permitted additives.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not on MHLW Designated Additives list; TBHQ has no approval in Japan

name: BHA
note: Synthetic antioxidant classified by IARC as possibly carcinogenic to humans based on animal studies. Common in US cereals and snack fats; EU permits it only in narrow uses.
maxPermittedLevel: Up to 200 mg/kg in fats and oils (EU)

name: BHT
note: Synthetic antioxidant with mixed animal-study findings. Common in US cereals and gum; more restricted in the EU.

name: Lecithins
note: Natural emulsifier from soy, sunflower or eggs. No safety concern.

name: Sodium lactate
note: Salt of lactic acid. No safety concern.

name: Citric acid
note: The acid of citrus fruits, produced by fermentation. No safety concern.

name: Sodium citrates
note: Salts of citric acid. No safety concern.

name: Potassium citrates
note: Salts of citric acid. No safety concern.

name: Calcium citrates
note: Salts of citric acid. No safety concern.

name: Tartaric acid
note: Grape-derived acid. No safety concern.

name: Potassium tartrates
note: Salt of tartaric acid. No safety concern.

name: Phosphoric acid
note: The acid in colas. EFSA's 2019 phosphate re-evaluation found total phosphate intake can exceed safe levels for high consumers, with bone and kidney health questions.

name: Sodium phosphates
note: Phosphate salts contributing to total phosphate intake, which EFSA flagged as exceeding safe levels for some consumers.

name: Potassium phosphates
note: Phosphate salts with the same total-intake concern flagged by EFSA.

name: Calcium phosphates
note: Phosphate salts with the same total-intake concern flagged by EFSA.

name: Rosemary extract
note: Natural antioxidant from rosemary. No safety concern at permitted levels.

regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Proposal pending (comments to Feb 2026); not yet on Permitted Preservatives list

name: Alginic acid
note: Seaweed-derived thickener. No safety concern.

name: Sodium alginate
note: Seaweed-derived thickener. No safety concern.

name: Agar
note: Seaweed-derived gelling agent. No safety concern.

name: Carrageenan
note: Seaweed-derived thickener debated for possible gut-inflammation effects; EFSA kept it approved but asked for more data. Common in US dairy and plant milks.
maxPermittedLevel: Often quantum satis (EU); not permitted in infant formula

name: Processed eucheuma seaweed
note: Semi-refined carrageenan with the same gut-effect debate.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Not separately listed; covered under carrageenan (whole-algae)
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Not separately listed; covered under carrageenan

name: Locust bean gum
note: Natural thickener from carob seeds. No safety concern. A 2026 EU regulation lowered its permitted level in infant foods for special medical purposes.

name: Guar gum
note: Natural thickener from guar beans. No safety concern at food levels. A 2026 EU regulation ends its use in infant foods for special medical purposes from April 2027.

name: Tragacanth
note: Natural gum; rare allergic reactions reported.

name: Acacia gum
note: Natural tree gum. No safety concern.

name: Xanthan gum
note: Fermentation-derived thickener. No safety concern at food levels.

name: Tara gum
note: Natural seed gum thickener. No safety concern.

name: Gellan gum
note: Fermentation-derived gelling agent. No safety concern.

name: Sorbitol
note: Sugar alcohol sweetener. Safe, but causes digestive upset in larger amounts.

name: Mannitol
note: Sugar alcohol sweetener with laxative effects in larger amounts.

name: Glycerol
note: Humectant and sweetener, safe at normal food levels. EFSA (2026) found a single slush ice drink can exceed the acute safe dose for young children and advised limits on glycerol in these drinks.

name: Polysorbate 80
note: Synthetic emulsifier; some studies suggest emulsifiers of this class may disturb gut bacteria and the gut lining.

name: Polysorbate 60
note: Synthetic emulsifier in the same class as polysorbate 80, with similar gut-microbiome questions.

name: Polysorbate 65
note: Synthetic emulsifier with the same class-level gut questions.

name: Pectin
note: Natural fruit-derived gelling agent. No safety concern. A 2026 EU regulation lowered its permitted level in infant foods for special medical purposes.

name: Diphosphates
note: Phosphate raising/texturing agents contributing to total phosphate intake, which EFSA flagged for high consumers.

name: Triphosphates
note: Phosphates used in processed meat and seafood; same total-intake concern.

name: Polyphosphates
note: Phosphates used in processed cheese and meats; same total-intake concern.

name: Cellulose
note: Plant fiber used for texture. No safety concern.

name: Methylcellulose
note: Modified plant fiber (common in meat substitutes). Low concern; a marker of heavy processing.

name: Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose
note: Modified cellulose thickener. Low concern; a marker of heavy processing.

name: Carboxymethylcellulose
note: Modified cellulose emulsifier; a controlled human study found it altered gut bacteria, prompting ongoing EFSA attention.

name: Mono- and diglycerides
note: Extremely common emulsifier made from fats. Low direct concern; part of the wider emulsifier-microbiome research debate.
synonyms: mono and diglycerides of fatty acids

name: DATEM
note: Dough-strengthening emulsifier in industrial bread. Low concern at permitted levels.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not on Japan's designated or existing food additives lists

synonyms: diacetyl tartaric acid esters of mono and diglycerides

name: Sucrose esters
note: Emulsifier from sugar and fats. Low concern within the acceptable intake.

name: Polyglycerol polyricinoleate
note: Emulsifier that lets chocolate makers use less cocoa butter. Low concern at permitted levels.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not on Japan's designated or existing food additives lists

name: Sodium stearoyl lactylate
note: Dough conditioner and emulsifier. Low concern at permitted levels.

name: Sorbitan monostearate
note: Emulsifier in yeast, chocolate and toppings. Low concern within acceptable intake.

name: Sodium carbonates
note: Baking soda and washing soda used as raising agents. No safety concern.

name: Potassium carbonates
note: Raising agent and acidity regulator. No safety concern.

name: Ammonium carbonates
note: Traditional raising agent that decomposes during baking. No safety concern.

name: Magnesium carbonates
note: Anti-caking agent. No safety concern.

name: Hydrochloric acid
note: Processing acid neutralized in the final food. No safety concern.

name: Calcium chloride
note: Firming agent for canned vegetables and cheese-making. No safety concern.

name: Magnesium chloride
note: Firming agent (also tofu coagulant). No safety concern.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Emulsifying and firming uses, including tofu

name: Calcium sulfate
note: Firming agent and flour treatment (also tofu coagulant). No safety concern.

name: Sodium hydroxide
note: Used in pretzel and olive processing; neutralized in the final food. No safety concern.

name: Calcium hydroxide
note: Traditional firming agent (e.g. corn nixtamalization). No safety concern.

name: Silicon dioxide
note: Anti-caking agent. EFSA asked for better data on nanoparticle content; low concern at typical levels.
maxPermittedLevel: Often quantum satis (EU); nanoparticle data under re-evaluation

name: Calcium silicate
note: Anti-caking agent with similar data gaps to other silicates.

name: Talc
note: Mineral anti-caking agent; food-grade talc must be asbestos-free, but the mineral family keeps it under scrutiny.

regionStatus.Canada: Restricted: Only for coating dried legumes/rice and as chewing gum filler

name: Glucono-delta-lactone
note: Slow-release acidifier for tofu and cured meats. No safety concern.

name: Glutamic acid
note: Flavor-enhancing amino acid. EFSA set a group intake level after re-evaluation; sensitive individuals report symptoms at high doses.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Not classified as a food additive in Canada; freely permitted

name: Monosodium glutamate
note: The most common flavor enhancer. Large reviews find it safe at food levels; EFSA set a conservative group intake after re-evaluation.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Not classified as a food additive in Canada; freely permitted

name: Disodium guanylate
note: Umami enhancer usually paired with MSG. Low concern; people with gout are sometimes advised to limit purines.

name: Disodium inosinate
note: Umami enhancer usually paired with MSG. Low concern at food levels.

name: Disodium 5'-ribonucleotides
note: Combined umami enhancer. Low concern at food levels.

name: Dimethylpolysiloxane
note: Anti-foaming silicone used in frying oils and drinks. Low concern at permitted levels.

name: Beeswax
note: Natural glazing agent. No safety concern.

name: Carnauba wax
note: Palm-leaf wax used to glaze candy and fruit. No safety concern.

name: Shellac
note: Natural resin glaze for candy and pills. No safety concern.

name: L-cysteine
note: Dough conditioner, often derived from feathers or hair. Low safety concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Designated only for bread dough conditioning and natural fruit juice antioxidant use
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Bread max 90 ppm; GMP in unstandardized bakery products

name: Potassium bromate
note: Flour improver NEVER approved in the EU and banned in most of the world after causing cancers in animal studies. Still legal in US flour and baked goods.

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Flour treatment agent, ~30 ppm max; residue must be decomposed/removed before final product
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Banned as a food additive since 1994

name: Azodicarbonamide
note: Dough conditioner BANNED in the EU (breakdown products include semicarbazide, a suspected carcinogen). Singapore bans it with fines; also prohibited in the UK and Australia. Still permitted in US bread and buns. The FDA opened a post-market review of azodicarbonamide in 2025.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not a designated food additive in Japan
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Flour treatment agent, 45 ppm of flour

name: Benzoyl peroxide
note: Flour-bleaching agent not permitted in the EU, where flour bleaching is generally prohibited. Common in US white flour.

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Designated only as diluted benzoyl peroxide for wheat flour
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Flour treatment agent, 150 ppm in flour

name: Brominated vegetable oil
note: Citrus-drink emulsifier never approved in the EU due to bromine accumulation in body fat; also banned in Japan and India. The US FDA finally revoked it in 2024 but stocks may persist.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Banned as a food additive since 2010
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Removed from permitted additives Aug 2024

name: Acesulfame potassium
note: Intense sweetener. EFSA's 2025 re-evaluation raised the acceptable daily intake to 15 mg/kg and found no safety concern at permitted levels; WHO still advises against non-sugar sweeteners for weight control.

name: Aspartame
note: Intense sweetener classified by IARC in 2023 as possibly carcinogenic (limited evidence); EFSA and JECFA maintain it is safe within the acceptable daily intake. Avoid if you have PKU.
maxPermittedLevel: 600 mg/L in soft drinks (EU); ADI 40 mg/kg bw/day (EFSA)

name: Cyclamates
note: Sweetener permitted in the EU within limits but banned in the US since 1970 over animal-study concerns.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Banned as a food additive since 1969
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not permitted in foods; sold only as a tabletop sweetener

name: Saccharin
note: The oldest intense sweetener. Early cancer scares were not confirmed in humans, but WHO advises limiting sweeteners generally.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Fully approved 2016 after decades as a restricted additive

name: Sucralose
note: Intense sweetener; questions remain about heated uses and gut-bacteria effects, and WHO advises against sweeteners for weight control.

name: Steviol glycosides
note: Plant-derived intense sweetener. Considered safe within the acceptable intake.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Listed as an existing (natural) food additive

name: Neotame
note: Aspartame-derived sweetener used in tiny amounts. EFSA's 2025 re-evaluation raised the acceptable daily intake to 10 mg/kg and found no safety concern at permitted levels.

name: Aspartame-acesulfame salt
note: Combination of aspartame and acesulfame K; carries the considerations of both.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not separately designated in Japan
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Approved as the combined salt

name: Maltitol
note: Sugar alcohol sweetener with laxative effects in larger amounts.

name: Xylitol
note: Sugar alcohol good for teeth; digestive upset in larger amounts (and highly toxic to dogs).

name: Erythritol
note: Sugar alcohol under new scrutiny after 2023 studies associated high blood levels with cardiovascular events; regulators are re-examining the data.

name: Quillaia extract
note: Natural foaming agent for drinks. Low concern at permitted levels.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Listed as an existing (natural) food additive
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Permitted as a foaming/emulsifying agent

name: Oxidized starch
note: Modified starch thickener. No safety concern.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Treated as a food ingredient rather than an additive

name: Distarch phosphate
note: Modified starch thickener. No safety concern.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Treated as a food ingredient rather than an additive

name: Acetylated distarch phosphate
note: Modified starch thickener. No safety concern.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Treated as a food ingredient rather than an additive

name: Acetylated starch
note: Modified starch thickener. No safety concern.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Treated as a food ingredient rather than an additive

name: Acetylated distarch adipate
note: Modified starch thickener; a marker of industrial processing more than a direct health risk.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Treated as a food ingredient rather than an additive

name: Hydroxypropyl distarch phosphate
note: Modified starch thickener; a marker of industrial processing.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Treated as a food ingredient rather than an additive

name: Starch sodium octenyl succinate
note: Emulsifying modified starch. Low concern at permitted levels. A 2026 EU regulation lowered its permitted level in infant foods for special medical purposes.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Treated as a food ingredient rather than an additive

name: Propylene glycol
note: Solvent and humectant the EU permits only as a carrier in narrow uses with strict limits, while it appears widely in US foods and flavorings.

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Designated but limited to a short list of foods (e.g. noodles) at low max levels
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Permitted as a carrier solvent for flavours

name: Canthaxanthin
note: Pigment restricted in the EU to one sausage type after high doses caused retinal deposits.

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Designated only for kamaboko fish-paste products
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Permitted food colour in bread, jam, ice cream, fish roe, etc.

name: Calcium benzoate
note: Benzoate preservative; same benzene-formation concern as sodium benzoate.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Only benzoic acid and sodium benzoate are designated
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Only benzoic acid and its Na/K salts are on the preservatives list

name: Ethylparaben (ester)
note: Paraben-type food preservative; permitted within group limits.

regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not on the permitted preservatives list

name: Biphenyl
note: Citrus-surface fungicide no longer permitted as an EU food additive.

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Permitted only as post-harvest fungicide on citrus
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not a permitted food additive

name: Orthophenyl phenol
note: Citrus-surface fungicide removed from the EU additive list; still used post-harvest on US citrus (as a pesticide).

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Permitted only as post-harvest fungicide (OPP) on citrus
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not on Canada's food additive lists

name: Sodium orthophenyl phenol
note: Salt of orthophenyl phenol, removed from the EU additive list.

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Permitted only as post-harvest fungicide (OPP-Na) on citrus
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not a permitted direct food additive

name: Hexamethylene tetramine
note: Preservative allowed in the EU only for one Italian cheese; releases small amounts of formaldehyde.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Absent from Japan's designated and existing additive lists
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not on Canada's List of Permitted Preservatives

name: Potassium acetate
note: Acidity regulator. No safety concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Absent from Japan's designated and existing additive lists

name: Sodium acetates
note: Vinegar salts (salt-and-vinegar flavor). No safety concern.

name: Calcium acetate
note: Acidity regulator. No safety concern.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Acidity regulator in beer and unstandardized foods

name: Boric acid
note: Permitted in the EU only for caviar; classified as toxic to reproduction.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Absent from Japan's designated and existing additive lists
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Health Canada treats it as not permitted in food

name: Borax (sodium tetraborate)
note: Permitted in the EU only for caviar; same reproductive-toxicity classification as boric acid.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Absent from Japan's designated and existing additive lists
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Treated as boric acid; not permitted in food

name: Calcium ascorbate
note: Vitamin C salt antioxidant. No safety concern.

name: Erythorbic acid
note: Vitamin C isomer used in cured meats to speed curing; low concern.

name: Sodium erythorbate
note: Common US cured-meat antioxidant; low concern in itself.

name: Adipic acid
note: Tart acidulant in gelatin desserts and drink powders; low concern.

name: Triammonium citrate
note: Acidity regulator; low concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not found on Japan's designated additives list
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Canada lists only the mono- and di-ammonium citrate forms

name: Calcium disodium EDTA
note: Chelator that keeps mayonnaise and canned goods from discoloring; strict acceptable intake, very common in US condiments.

name: Propylene glycol alginate
note: Foam stabilizer (salad dressings, beer); contributes to propylene glycol exposure.

name: Karaya gum
note: Natural tree gum; rare allergic reactions.

name: Konjac
note: Gelling fiber BANNED in EU jelly mini-cups after choking deaths; fine in other foods.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Classified as a traditional food (konnyaku), not an additive
regionStatus.Canada: Restricted: Mini-cup jelly banned; sold as food ingredient, not on additive lists

name: Polysorbate 20
note: Synthetic emulsifier; same class-level gut-microbiome questions as polysorbate 80.

regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Absent from Canada's lists; only polysorbates 60/65/80 approved

name: Sucrose acetate isobutyrate
note: Citrus-drink cloud stabilizer that replaced BVO in many US sodas; EFSA set an acceptable intake.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not among Japan's designated food additives
regionStatus.Canada: Restricted: Density-adjusting agent in citrus/spruce beverages only

name: Glycerol ester of wood rosin
note: Another BVO replacement in US citrus drinks; permitted with limits.

name: Beta-cyclodextrin
note: Starch-derived flavor carrier; low concern.

regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Only alpha-cyclodextrin is on Canada's lists; beta form absent

name: Hydroxypropyl cellulose
note: Modified cellulose thickener; a processing marker.

name: Sodium/potassium/calcium salts of fatty acids
note: Soap-like emulsifiers from fats. No safety concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Calcium stearate broad; sodium/potassium salts narrowly permitted
regionStatus.Canada: Restricted: Na/K stearate limited to chewing gum/stabilizer uses

name: Magnesium salts of fatty acids
note: Anti-caking fat salts (magnesium stearate). No safety concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Restricted: Magnesium stearate limited to capsules/tablets/tablet confectionery
regionStatus.Canada: Restricted: Limited to confectionery release agent and tablet binder uses

name: Acetic acid esters of mono/diglycerides
note: Emulsifier family; low concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Covered by Japan's glycerin fatty acid ester category

name: Lactic acid esters of mono/diglycerides
note: Emulsifier family; low concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Covered by Japan's glycerin fatty acid ester category

name: Citric acid esters of mono/diglycerides
note: Emulsifier family; low concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Covered by Japan's glycerin fatty acid ester category

name: Polyglycerol esters of fatty acids
note: Cake and topping emulsifier; low concern within limits.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Regulated as a designated glycerin fatty acid ester

name: Propylene glycol esters of fatty acids
note: Whipping-aid emulsifier; contributes to propylene glycol exposure.

name: Sorbitan tristearate
note: Chocolate and topping emulsifier; low concern within limits.

name: Potassium chloride
note: Salt substitute. No concern at food levels.

name: Stannous chloride
note: Tin salt permitted only for canned white asparagus in the EU; tin intake is the concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not found on Japan's designated or existing additive lists
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Colour stabilizer in canned asparagus and soft drinks, 25 ppm as tin

name: Aluminium sulphate
note: Firming agent restricted in the EU as part of cutting total aluminium exposure; still in some US pickled products and baking powders.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not found on Japan's designated or existing additive lists
regionStatus.Canada: Restricted: Since Oct 2025 narrowed to modified-starch manufacturing uses

name: Sodium ferrocyanide
note: Anti-caking agent in salt; despite the name, the cyanide is locked in a stable complex.

name: Potassium ferrocyanide
note: Anti-caking agent in salt; stable, low concern.

name: Sodium aluminium phosphate
note: US baking-powder leavening acid; the EU removed most uses to cut aluminium exposure.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not found on Japan's designated or existing additive lists
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: All permitted uses removed Oct 2025 after Health Canada's aluminum review

name: Sodium aluminium silicate
note: Anti-caking agent; EU restricted aluminium-containing additives in 2014.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not found on Japan's designated or existing additive lists

name: 4-Hexylresorcinol
note: Prevents shrimp black spot; permitted with residue limits.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not found on Japan's designated or existing additive lists
regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Preservative on crustaceans, residue max 1 ppm

name: Candelilla wax
note: Plant wax glazing agent. No concern.

name: Microcrystalline wax
note: Petroleum-derived glaze restricted in the EU to a few uses (gum, melon and fruit surfaces).

regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not on Canada's glazing and polishing agent list

name: Thaumatin
note: Sweet protein from the katemfe fruit. No concern.

name: Neohesperidin DC
note: Citrus-derived intense sweetener; low concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Not permitted: Not found on Japan's positive lists
regionStatus.Canada: Not permitted: Not on Canada's permitted sweeteners list

name: Lactitol
note: Sugar alcohol; laxative in larger amounts.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Treated as a food ingredient, not a designated additive

name: Advantame
note: Ultra-potent aspartame-derived sweetener used in tiny amounts; considered safe within intake limits.

regionStatus.Canada: Restricted: Permitted only in certain unstandardized foods/beverages

name: Lysozyme
note: Egg-derived enzyme preservative (cheese, wine); an egg allergen.

name: Polydextrose
note: Synthetic fiber and bulking agent; laxative only in large amounts.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Treated as a general food ingredient, not a designated additive

name: Polyvinylpyrrolidone
note: Clarifier and tablet binder; EU restricts it to limited uses.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Fining agent in beer, cider and wine, residue max 2 ppm

name: Monostarch phosphate
note: Modified starch thickener. No safety concern.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Covered via permitted starch-modifying agents (List 13)

name: Hydroxypropyl starch
note: Modified starch; a processing marker.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Covered via permitted starch-modifying agents (List 13)

name: Triethyl citrate
note: Foam stabilizer for egg products; low concern.

regionStatus.Canada: Permitted: Whipping agent in egg white products

name: Triacetin
note: Glycerin-derived flavor carrier; low concern.

regionStatus.Japan: Permitted: Regulated as a designated glycerin acetate ester
